params.text:
Planning the journey, not just the visit

Most of your working day happens outside the front door. Travel time is work time, and the risks in it — unfamiliar streets, bad weather, tight rotas, a car that lets you down — are things to plan for, not things to absorb. HSE guidance on lone working (INDG73) treats getting to and from the job as part of the job, so your employer should support you to do it safely.

  • Know the route before you set off: check the address, postcode quirks and any access notes in the care plan, so you are not sitting in a dark street scrolling your phone.
  • Park with your exit in mind: pick a lit, open spot you can pull straight out of. Reverse in where you can, and avoid tucking yourself down an alley or behind garages.
  • Keep the vehicle roadworthy: fuel or charge, tyres, lights, oil and screenwash checked regularly. A breakdown is a personal-safety problem as much as a mechanical one.
  • Carry a basic kit: charged phone and power bank, torch, warm layer, water and a hi-vis in the boot.
  • Drive for the conditions, not the rota: if you are running late, ring the office. No call is worth taking a bend too fast.
  • If you break down: get clear of the carriageway if you can, stay visible, and ring your breakdown provider and the office with your exact location. On a fast road, leave by the nearside door and wait behind a barrier. If it is safer to stay in the car, keep the doors locked.

params.alt: A care worker checking a route on a phone before setting off

params.text:
On foot, on the bus, and on your guard

Suzy Lamplugh Trust personal-safety principles come down to staying aware, looking purposeful and trusting your instinct. Being careful does not mean looking nervous.

  • Walk like you know where you are going: head up, hands free, hood down and headphones out, especially in the dark.
  • Do not advertise the job: carry your ID, but tuck the lanyard away between calls. A uniform and a bag can suggest you are carrying medication, keys or cash.
  • Leave nothing on show: bags, phone, PPE and any medication go in the boot, out of sight, and never stay in the car overnight.
  • Public transport: wait in lit, busy areas, sit near the driver or other passengers, and plan the last stretch on foot as carefully as the bus route itself.
  • If you think you are being followed: cross over, change direction and head for a shop, pub or busy building rather than your next visit. Ring someone and say out loud where you are. If you feel threatened, call 999.

Anything that frightened you gets reported and recorded, even when nothing actually happened. Near misses are what shape the next risk assessment.

params.question: Your car breaks down on an unlit country lane between two evening calls. What is the right first move?

answers.text: Make yourself visible and safe, then ring your breakdown provider and the office with your exact location, staying locked in the car if it is safer to remain there.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Visibility first, then get help on the way and make sure someone at base knows precisely where you are and that your remaining calls need covering.

answers.text: Set off on foot to the next service user, since it is only about fifteen minutes away and they are expecting you.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Walking an unlit lane alone, in uniform, carrying a bag leaves you exposed and nobody knows where you are. Get help to you instead.

answers.text: Sit quietly in the dark car and try to sort the fault yourself so you do not lose visit time.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. An unlit, unmarked vehicle is a collision risk, and losing visit time is the office's problem to solve, not yours to fix at the roadside.

answers.text: Accept a lift from the first driver who pulls over and deal with the car in the morning.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Getting into a stranger's vehicle removes every safeguard you have. Wait for the breakdown service or a colleague your office has arranged.
